summary:
The Red Guards were mass paramilitary youth organizations mobilized by Mao Zedong beginning in August 1966 as the shock troops of the [[event:2689|Cultural Revolution]]. Drawn primarily from high school and university students, Red Guard groups were encouraged to attack the Four Olds — old customs, old culture, old habits, and old ideas — and to denounce teachers, intellectuals, party officials, and anyone else identified as insufficiently revolutionary. Mao received millions of young Red Guards at mass rallies in Tiananmen Square in the summer and autumn of 1966, personally validating their mission.

Red Guard units became vehicles for settling personal scores, factional rivalry, and ideological competition. They destroyed cultural and religious artifacts, vandalized temples and historic sites, ransacked private homes, and subjected victims to public humiliation sessions. Universities and schools were closed for years. As different Red Guard factions began fighting each other in 1967–1968, Mao deployed the People's Liberation Army to restore order and eventually sent millions of Red Guards to the countryside for forced rural labor — the so-called Up to the Mountains and Down to the Villages movement — effectively disbanding the movement. The generation of youth who served as Red Guards later became known in China as the lost generation.

read_more_links.title: Up to the Mountains and Down to the Villages
